posts.author: Foxyy
posts.body: @AyyLmaonnaise Calling Cezary Oleksiejczuk slow is kinda crazy, dudes known for his super quick striking. Honestly Michal and Cezary clearly both are more on the fast twitch/ explosive punching side of the spectrum. Cezary is a lot different than his brother though, hes long, kicks well, has a strong jab and a blistering cross. He works in straight punches and counters well, but he can rip hooks in close and oddly has a good lead uppercut he fits in. Michal is all offense, loops his punches and is just entirely different in build and approach. Cezary has done his last couple camps down in Brazil with the fighting Nerds, not sure how much that gym will help his defensive wrestling where he came up short vs Alibeg Rasulov but maybe it will surprise us. Cezary has been putting in rounds with Poatan and Borralho that I've seen and I mean legit, hard sparring, quality 1 on 1 in the cage rounds not open mat pitter patter MMA sparring. That said, besides the Breese fight, Cezary has fought his career at 170 (notoriously quick at 170 too) and looked visibly small vs Breese when he went up in his last bout, so Haigs size paired with being a grappling centric guy could pose difficulty, Cezary is long and tall but hes not muscular and hes all legs, with a short narrow torso ..was noticeably smaller when getting rounds in with Caio (who he was clearly faster than lol). Im not really sure what's ideal weight division wise for him, but he might want to look to add some muscle and grow into 185 if he stays there. I think he beats Haig, the level of experience being 15-3 pro and 24-7 amateur, having notable wins vs guys like Aigun Akhmetov and Tom Breese is just a lot vs an older, less experienced Haig.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.body: On a recent evening, Pasha Boyer, a thirty-two-year-old father and professional sports bettor who lives in Missoula, Montana, logged on to TikTok to continue what has become an unpaid full-time job: debating Israel vs. Palestine over TikTok Live. As usual, his screen resembled a nightmarish Zoom call, with the seven other interlocutors locked in a perpetual state of disagreement. “They said literally everything I was saying was wrong,” he told me. The acrimony did not deter him. He stayed on hour after hour, as fourteen hundred viewers came and went, and he eventually settled into a debate with a fellow-Jew who disagreed with Boyer’s pro-Israel stance. By the time Boyer logged off, it was 3 p.m. the next day; eighteen hours had elapsed. “You know what the crazy part is?” he said. “The other guy was on until three o’clock in the afternoon as well.” With TikTok’s cultural influence well established, many of its users are now exploring whether the platform can host constructive debates. Boyer is one of several dozen people who have pushed this question to its limit, hashing out one of the world’s most contentious and intractable issues on TikTok Live, the app’s most freewheeling feature. Whether Boyer and his fellow-debaters can change minds is unclear. But, given the opportunity to engage with real people in front of large audiences, staunchly pro-Israel and pro-Palestine users have decided to join live streams that allow them to express their views—and argue with others—seemingly forever.

posts.author: Foxyy
posts.body: Aljo and Zalal are the main event this weekend. Aljo's got a lot of championship experience, and this is Zalal's first main event. This fight is at the apex; I guess Zalal is such a star they're actively afraid to put him in front of a crowd. Riveting stuff. Looking at this fight, Aljo has the clear wrestling, pressure, and experience advantage, while Zalal has a clear advantage in striking, movement, and general well-roundedness. From a gameplanning perspective, I think they're about even; both fighters are going to come in with proper gameplans. To state the obvous, Aljo's win condition here is consistently taking Zalal down and controlling him while Zalal's win condition is outstriking Aljo on the feet. Aljo will need to close distance in order to take Zalal down, and Zalal will need to deploy a movement-heavy gameplan to make it harder for Aljo to close and shoot. To take away Zalal's movement, Aljo will pressure, use the small apex cage, and low kicks. To keep the fight on the feet, Zalal will be continuously sticking and moving, and feinting with knees. I expect the fight to start out rather slow, with both fighters feeling things out. Zalal will likely win minutes early, and once Aljo shoots, the fight begins. I expect the most pivotal work to be done in rounds 2 and 3. I think Aljo will have some difficulty getting Zalal down and controlling him, and when he gets there, he won't be able to dominate Zalal in my opinion. I also think it will be somewhat difficult for Zalal to effectively manage distance during this time. As the fight progresses, I expect Zalal to build. Aljo is older now and his cardio doesn't fare well here theoretically. With the work he'll have to put in to get Zalal down, he will tire out. He will have to use his massive experience edge here. I expect the first half of the fight to be quite competitive, with the second half being in favor of Zalal. Conclusion: Aljo will need to rely on winning 1-3. Zalal will need to do good work in the first half of the fight and take advantage of his late edge. I see Zalal winning rounds 4-5 at a high clip here. The most likely outcome here in my opinion is Zalal winning 3-2 or 4-1. Aljo has an outside shot, but to be honest I think everything has to go right for him to win. Prediction: Zalal by decision. I think this is a pivotal fight for both fighters. Zalal will now he in the near-title picture, likely 1 win away. Aljo will be over 35 years old and outside of the top 5. It will be difficult for him to find the confidence to push for the title further if he were to lose here.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.body: Curtis Blaydes is the most dominant fundamental wrestler the division has ever seen, possessing a double-leg takedown so explosive and technically sound that it feels like an inevitability rather than a strike. Blaydes doesn't just compete; he imposes a grueling, high-pressure pace that suffocates opponents, blending world-class grappling with a vastly improved striking game. His ability to chain wrestling sequences together while maintaining the gas tank of a middleweight makes him a nightmare matchup for anyone in the world, proving time and again that he is the ultimate "gatekeeper to the gold" who consistently shuts down the hype of rising contenders. Beyond the raw physicality, there is a profound level of resilience and tactical intelligence in Blaydes’ game. He has navigated one of the toughest schedules in UFC history, never shying away from the most dangerous knockout artists in the sport. While others might play it safe, Blaydes trusts in his "Razor" elbows and his ability to neutralize chaos with calculated control. He is a true professional who stays out of the drama and lets his work inside the Octagon do the talking. Whether he’s setting records for the most takedowns in heavyweight history or showing off his evolved kickboxing, Blaydes remains a perennial title threat and a masterclass in how to weaponize elite wrestling in MMA.

posts.body: One of my fav fighters right now is Youssef Zalal. He fights Aljo this weekend. Super pumped. Odds are good too. I may post a breakdown in here at some point

posts.author: CheeseInTheClinch
posts.body: Aljo and Zalal are the main event this weekend. Aljo's got a lot of championship experience, and this is Zalal's first main event. This fight is at the apex; I guess Zalal is such a star they're actively afraid to put him in front of a crowd. Riveting stuff. Looking at this fight, Aljo has the clear wrestling, pressure, and experience advantage, while Zalal has a clear advantage in striking, movement, and general well-roundedness. From a gameplanning perspective, I think they're about even; both fighters are going to come in with proper gameplans. To state the obvous, Aljo's win condition here is consistently taking Zalal down and controlling him while Zalal's win condition is outstriking Aljo on the feet. Aljo will need to close distance in order to take Zalal down, and Zalal will need to deploy a movement-heavy gameplan to make it harder for Aljo to close and shoot. To take away Zalal's movement, Aljo will pressure, use the small apex cage, and low kicks. To keep the fight on the feet, Zalal will be continuously sticking and moving, and feinting with knees. I expect the fight to start out rather slow, with both fighters feeling things out. Zalal will likely win minutes early, and once Aljo shoots, the fight begins. I expect the most pivotal work to be done in rounds 2 and 3. I think Aljo will have some difficulty getting Zalal down and controlling him, and when he gets there, he won't be able to dominate Zalal in my opinion. I also think it will be somewhat difficult for Zalal to effectively manage distance during this time. As the fight progresses, I expect Zalal to build. Aljo is older now and his cardio doesn't fare well here theoretically. With the work he'll have to put in to get Zalal down, he will tire out. He will have to use his massive experience edge here. I expect the first half of the fight to be quite competitive, with the second half being in favor of Zalal. Conclusion: Aljo will need to rely on winning 1-3. Zalal will need to do good work in the first half of the fight and take advantage of his late edge. I see Zalal winning rounds 4-5 at a high clip here. The most likely outcome here in my opinion is Zalal winning 3-2 or 4-1. Aljo has an outside shot, but to be honest I think everything has to go right for him to win. Prediction: Zalal by decision. I think this is a pivotal fight for both fighters. Zalal will now he in the near-title picture, likely 1 win away. Aljo will be over 35 years old and outside of the top 5. It will be difficult for him to find the confidence to push for the title further if he were to lose here.

posts.author: CheeseInTheClinch
posts.body: Manatees are gentle, slow-moving marine mammals often called “sea cows,” known for their peaceful temperament, curious personalities, and relaxed way of life. They spend much of their time grazing on seagrass and other aquatic vegetation in warm rivers, springs, estuaries, and coastal waters. Despite their large size, they are graceful swimmers and remarkably agile in the water. Manatees are air-breathing mammals closely related to elephants, and they are recognized for their whiskered snouts, paddle-shaped tails, and calm, non-aggressive behavior, which has made them one of the most beloved marine animals in the world.

posts.author: Foxyy
posts.body: Matt Schnell, DECI see erceg as schnell but with a better chin; both are pretty good grapplers but love to strike. Both guys have questionable fight iq and suspect defense (Erceg 0 head movement and schnell no chin). Erceg has looked solid thus far in the ufc but not sure he’s a contender yet. His striking is clean, sticks to the basics and is just a very sharp, technical striker. He has some good power but is a really bad finisher, usually going to the ground after dropping his opponents. What I hate about his striking is the lack of head movement. Now schnell has a glass jaw so he is more of a liability, but erceg’s head doesn’t move at all. He has shown a very good chin but has been rocked numerous times against much lesser comp than schnell. I get he’s very chinny but schnells striking is much better than anyone gives it credit for. He is a dawg and just walks yo down brawling for 15 minutes. I think Erceg is much more technical but schnells forward pressure could give him a lot of issues, especially without head movmemets. I really like erceg’s grappling, his back takes are very slick and he has some nasty sub attempts. However, he does make his fair share of mistakes and doesn’t have amazing control. Schnell is pretty capable on the ground though so doubt we see much here. Much like loik vs alselwady, I want to wait for weigh ins because there’s a good chance schnell has a **** cut again; he has been struggling to make weight a lot recently and has been super inactive. If he shows up though I think he can beat Erceg. Erceg is good but pretty flawed imo, someone has to expose it eventually

posts.author: Josefaldo
posts.body: The heavyweight division is known for having it’s fair share of rotund creatures, from pigs and hogs, to hippos, elephants… And many more in between, it has been known as the home of all pachyderms with a fighting spirit, however, not all morbidly obese creatures have the habit or even instinct to fight, matter of fact, most not only dislike the practice but also are terribly bad at it, the manatee, a marine mammal commonly found in tropical waters and at blueflow research center in the north west of the USA, perfectly encapsulates this two factors. Having no natural predators, the manatee evolved into a peaceful creature that isn’t very capable of inflicting violence upon others, this did not stop a young manatee born in the shores of Australia of wishing to become a fighter, as a calf, he always dreamed of being able to throw leg kicks with his tail at range and blobbing his opponents with his flippers in a similar way of a jab, however, there was no one to actually train him due to the fact that kickboxing coaches usually habit the land and are incapable of fighting under water (see Kimbo Slice VS Dada 5000 for when that happens) so this hungry and determined creature made it’s way into land, in the hopes of achieving his dream: to blob his way into being a champion, his name: Tai Tuivasa. In any other weight class the road in which our hero traveled thru would be a hard one, especially when we are talking about creatures not known for combat (See Gerald “the tired cow” Meerschaert losing to mma legend Jay Ellis), however, by the time Tai started his mma career, most great heavyweights were already on their way to retirement while he was on his way in and out of fast food chains, as such he didn’t face any real opposition until the day he entered the then largest MMA event in the world, the UFC, where after a quick string of wins, his blobbelous ways would be exploited by fighters who happened to have actual skills or weren’t insanely old, two things rarely seem in the modern day heavyweight division, bellow a picture of Tai unwinding with Xicão after 15 minutes of live, uncut fatness. Where is he now? Geopolitical struggle: In more recent years, Tai has been the subject of deep controversy, when he clogged the straight of Hormuz by swimming in it, which required a joint effort from many different world powers to remove him from that body of water and allow for world trade to continue, Tai refused to elaborate on the recent Iran VS USA war and the closure of the Hormuz Straight by Iran. MMA Career: After suffering 3 straight losses, Tai was then matched with other lesser dangerous creatures such as aging giraffes and pigs who escaped the pigsty, this made so he was able to get a winstreak going, surprisingly he was able to then beat actual good heavyweights in Derrick Lewis and Augusto Sakai, this granted him a title elimination bout against french paw patrol star Cyril “no eyes can see” Gane, unfortunately for our hero, Gane had actual fighting skills and not only that, but less than 35% body fat, which swiftly led to a KO loss for Tai, soon afterwards, Tai started to revert back into his gentle instincts, which prevented him from ever winning again, but allowed him to reach enormous sizes (or regular for a manatee). Tattoos: Recently Tai was spotted having a “Jiu Jitsu” tattoo on his back, which sparked controversy among all who had two functioning eyes and had seem him being blobbed by all manner of Heavyweight white belts, including Louie Suthercan, a pig shapped can from the UK who did not know leg locks were a thing until he fell victim to Valter “the pig paw snatcher” Walker yet was able to blob Tai for over 3 rounds, strategically utilizing his belly fat as a counter weight, preventing Tai to flop back on his tail.
